description:
README.md's "Plugin System" section documents only the LEGACY interface (auth-setup/auth-check/push/sync + push/sync response schemas). The entire native-protocol line of work — describe/propose subcommands, field projections, structured conflict reporting, the reject veto branch, the create/drop lifecycle events, and the describe-gated EventCtx side channel (wants_context + --ctx-file) — is documented only in docs/SPEC-lifecycle-sync-participants.md, which is a design/contract spec, not plugin-author-facing onboarding docs.

WHY THIS MATTERS: a plugin author reading README today cannot discover the native protocol at all; they'd write a legacy push/sync plugin and never learn they can declare projections, return structured conflicts, veto a transition, observe create/drop, or request EventCtx. The SPEC is authoritative but reads as internal architecture, not a how-to. README is where an integrator looks first.

SCOPE: a new README subsection (or docs/PLUGINS.md linked from README) covering the native protocol as an author would consume it: the describe response shape (subscriptions, projection {owns/reads/external_prefixes}, retry_budget, wants_context), the propose request/response (ok/conflict/reject), the per-event semantics, create/drop gating rules (incl. required/gating-on-drop being a config error), and the EventCtx v1 schema delivered via --ctx-file. Cross-link the SPEC for the formal contract; do not duplicate it. This is a pre-existing documentation gap (predates epic bl-cc24 by several balls); bl-cc24 only extended the protocol the README never covered.
title: README: document the native plugin protocol (describe/propose, projections, reject, create/drop, EventCtx) [bl-c972]
